title: Three Weeks In — The Mite Wash
excerpt: Three weeks after install is when a new package colony crosses a line. The queen has been laying long enough that you have real capped brood — which means mites have places to hide and reproduce. We ran an alcohol wash on both hives today, and the numbers came back clean.
alt: Sawyer's hive brood frame during the three-week inspection

title: Opening the Books — Our First Full Inspection
excerpt: Eleven days after install, the weather finally lined up — sunny, a light breeze, warm enough that the bees were foraging hard. The colonies were calm from the moment we cracked the first cover.
alt: First full hive inspection at Bootstrap Beekeeping apiary

title: They Are Bringing It Home
excerpt: You do not need to open a hive to know things are going well. Sometimes you just watch the landing board. Those bright yellow pollen baskets tell the whole story.
alt: Foragers returning to the hive loaded with pollen

title: Day 3 — A Quick Look and a Good Sign
excerpt: Day three called for a quick, low-disturbance check — just enough to confirm both queens had been released and that things were moving in the right direction.
alt: Frames covered in bees during day 3 queen check

title: 24 Hours In — So Far, So Good
excerpt: By the next morning both hives had bees moving in and out of the entrance with purpose. Not frantic, not clustered in confusion — just working.
alt: Both hives active 24 hours after install

title: The First Shake — Installing Our Packages
excerpt: There is something that happens the moment you tip a package of bees and feel that low, collective hum transfer through your gloves and up your arms. Nobody warns you about that part.
alt: Package install day at the Bootstrap Beekeeping apiary
